claim: The 15Al alloy (nominal Fe-35Mn-15Al-5Cu) had measured composition Fe-44.6Mn-35.2Al-15.0Cu-5.2 (at.%); the 17Al alloy had Fe-43.2Mn-34.8Al-17.0Cu-5.0; the 19Al alloy had Fe-41.6Mn-34.8Al-18.6Cu-5.0.
evidence_text: Table 1 Chemical compositions of the 15Al, 17Al and 19Al alloys. 15Al 44.6 35.2 15.0 5.2 17Al 43.2 34.8 17.0 5.0 19Al 41.6 34.8 18.6 5.0

claim: The alloys were homogenized at 1200 °C for 24 h, hot-rolled to 4 mm thickness, solution treated at 1100 °C for 10 min followed by oil quenching, and aged at temperatures between 525 °C and 650 °C for 1, 3, 6, and 10 h followed by water quenching.
evidence_text: The ingots were homogenized at 1200 ◦C for 24 h, then hot-rolled into plates with a thickness of 4 mm. Subsequently, these plates were cut into 10 × 5 × 2 mm3 samples using electrical discharge machining for Vickers hardness measurements and microstructure characterizations. The samples were solution treated at 1100 ◦C for 10 min under an argon atmosphere, followed by oil quenching. The solution-treated samples were then aged at temperatures between 525 ◦C and 650 ◦C for 1, 3, 6 and 10 h, followed by water quenching.

claim: The densities measured by Archimedes method were 6.85 g/cm³ for 15Al, 6.76 g/cm³ for 17Al, and 6.66 g/cm³ for 19Al.
evidence_text: The densities of 15Al, 17Al and 19Al alloys were measured by Archimedes method as 6.85, 6.76, 6.66 g·cm-3.

claim: The 15Al alloy achieved a peak Vickers hardness of 965.6 ± 7.6 HV after aging at 600 °C for 6 h.
The 17Al alloy achieved a peak Vickers hardness of 924.9 ± 25.8 HV after aging at 575 °C for 6 h.
The 19Al alloy achieved a peak Vickers hardness of 917.8 ± 24.5 HV after aging at 600 °C for 10 h.
The solution-treated 15Al alloy had a Vickers hardness of 253.4 ± 2.1 HV.
evidence_text: Solution-treated 253.4 ± 2.1

claim: The solution-treated 17Al alloy had a Vickers hardness of 259.8 ± 1.5 HV.
evidence_text: Solution-treated 259.8 ± 1.5

claim: The solution-treated 19Al alloy had a Vickers hardness of 263.8 ± 1.1 HV.
evidence_text: Solution-treated 263.8 ± 1.1

claim: The Fe-35.2Mn-15Al-5.2Cu low-density steel achieved an ultra-high specific hardness of 141 HV·cm³/g.
evidence_text: our Fe-35.2Mn-15Al-5.2Cu low-density steel achieved an ultra-high specific hardness of 141 HV·cm3 /g.

claim: In prior Fe-Mn-Al low-density steels, the maximum hardness achieved by β-Mn precipitation was typically below 800 HV, e.g., Fe-31Mn-11Al-0.89C steel reached 750 HV after aging at 550 °C for over 1000 min.
evidence_text: the hardening effect of β-Mn precipitated in Fe-Mn-Al low-density steels is still limited, with the maximum hardness typically falling below 800 HV [24,25]. Furthermore, the slow precipitation rate of β-Mn during aging would extend the aging time for achieving high hardness. For instance, the maximum hardness value achieved in Fe-31Mn-11Al-0.89C steel was 750 HV after aging at 550 ◦C for over 1000 min [24].

claim: Fracture toughness KIC was measured using indentation technique, calculated by Eq. (1) with elastic modulus E (164 GPa for 15Al, 175 GPa for 17Al, 181 GPa for 19Al), Vickers hardness HV, indentation load P (9.8 N), and average crack length c, as illustrated in Fig. 1.
evidence_text: The fracture toughness (KIC) values of the Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with peak hardness values (HV = 965.6 for 15Al, HV = 924.9 for 17Al, and HV = 917.8 for 19Al) were measured using the indentation technique [28]. The fracture toughness of the samples was evaluated based on the crack propagation. KIC was calculated using Eq. (1): ... where E is the elastic modulus (164 GPa for 15Al, 175 GPa for 17Al and 181 GPa for 19Al measured through compressive stress-strain curves), HV is the Vickers hardness, P is the indentation load (9.8 N), and c is the average crack length

claim: The addition of 1 wt.% Al reduces the density of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels by approximately 0.1 g/cm³.
evidence_text: The addition of 1 wt.% Al reduces the density by approximately 0.1 g/cm3 [21,27].

claim: The 19Al alloy reached a peak hardness of 917.8 HV after aging at 600 °C for 10 h.
evidence_text: 917.8 HV for the 19Al alloy after aging at 600 ◦C for 10 h

claim: At aging temperatures of 575 °C and above, the aging hardening response was significantly accelerated, with a substantial increase in hardness after just 1 h.
evidence_text: At temperatures of 575 ◦C and above, the aging hardening response was significantly accelerated. The hardness showed a substantial increase after just 1 h of aging

claim: At aging temperatures from 525 °C to 575 °C, the aging hardening response of the alloys significantly slowed down with increasing Al content.
evidence_text: At the aging temperatures from 525 ◦C to 575 ◦C, the aging hardening response of the alloys significantly slowed down with increasing Al content.

claim: The optimal aging temperature range for achieving ultra-high hardness shifted to higher temperatures with increasing Al content: 525–600 °C for 15Al, 550–600 °C for 17Al, and 575–625 °C for 19Al.
evidence_text: the optimal aging temperature range for achieving ultra-high hardness for 15Al alloy was mainly in the range of 525 ◦C-600 ◦C. However, the optimal aging temperature range to achieve such ultra-high hardness increased with increasing Al content, primarily in the range of 550 ◦C-600 ◦C for 17Al alloy and 575 ◦C-625 ◦C for 19Al alloy.

claim: After aging the 15Al alloy at 550 °C for 6 h, the α matrix had almost entirely transformed into β-Mn, and a new fine BCC phase was present within the β-Mn.
evidence_text: After aging at 550 ◦C for 6 h, the α matrix had almost entirely transformed into β-Mn. Furthermore, a new fine BCC phase was present within the β-Mn.

claim: In the 17Al alloy aged at 525 °C for 10 h, only 20.5% β-Mn formed along α grain boundaries and within α grains, together with some small α-Mn particles.
evidence_text: after aging the 17Al alloy at 525 ◦C for 10 h, only a small amount of β-Mn (20.5 %) formed along the α grain boundaries and within the α grains. In addition, some small α-Mn particles presented in the α matrix

claim: In the 17Al alloy aged at 575 °C for 6 h and at 650 °C for 10 h, the α matrix almost completely transformed into β-Mn, and new precipitates with BCC and FCC crystalline structures were present within the β-Mn.
evidence_text: In samples aged at 575 ◦C for 6 h and at 650 ◦C for 10 h, the α matrix had almost completely transformed into β-Mn. Besides, a small amount of new precipitates with BCC (Red highlighted) and FCC (Pink highlighted) crystalline structures were present within the β-Mn.

claim: After aging the 19Al alloy at 525 °C for 10 h, only 12.1% β-Mn precipitated, resulting in a minimal increase in hardness.
evidence_text: after aging the 19Al alloy at 525 ◦C for 10 h, only a small amount of β-Mn precipitated (12.1 %), resulting in a minimal increase in hardness.

claim: SE images showed α-Mn precipitates as fine particles, while short rod-shaped β-Mn phase precipitated along α grain boundaries and interior of α grains, gradually growing until contact.
evidence_text: The α-Mn precipitates were dispersed in the α matrix as fine particles. The short rod-shaped β-Mn phase precipitated along α grain boundaries and the interior of α grains, and gradually grew until the precipitates came into contact with each other.

claim: The rate of precipitation and growth for β-Mn was accelerated with increasing aging temperatures, partly attributed to enhanced atomic diffusion rate; at 575 °C, a significant amount of β-Mn formed after just 1 h.
evidence_text: the rate of precipitation and growth for β-Mn was accelerated with increasing aging temperatures, partly attributing to the enhanced atomic diffusion rate. At 575 ◦C, a significant amount of β-Mn was formed after just 1 h of aging.

claim: Increasing Al content slowed down the precipitation kinetics of β-Mn during low-temperature aging; at 525 °C, β-Mn precipitation in 17Al and 19Al was very slow compared to 15Al, with only a small amount observed even after 10 h.
evidence_text: It is evident that the increase in Al content slowed down the precipitation kinetics of β-Mn during the low-temperature aging processes. When 17Al and 19Al alloys were aged at 525 ◦C, the precipitation of β-Mn was very slow as compared to the 15Al alloy, and only a small amount of β-Mn was observed even after 10 h of aging.

claim: At 550 °C, the α matrix had nearly fully transformed into β-Mn in the 17Al alloy after 6 h of aging, whereas the 19Al alloy required 10 h.
evidence_text: At 550 ◦C, the α matrix had nearly fully transformed into β-Mn in the 17Al alloy after 6 h of aging. As for the 19Al alloy, it required 10 h.

claim: XRD patterns (Fig. 10) of 15Al aged at 550 °C for 1 h, 17Al aged at 575 °C for 1 h, and 19Al aged at 550 °C for 6 h all contained both α-Mn and β-Mn diffraction peaks, indicating their co-precipitation before complete β-Mn transformation.
evidence_text: the 15Al alloy aged at 550 ◦C for 1 h, 17Al alloy aged at 575 ◦C for 1 h, and 19Al alloy aged at 550 ◦C for 6 h were selected for XRD tests, and the results are shown in Fig. 10. Both α-Mn and β-Mn diffraction peaks were found in these XRD patterns. This indicates that α-Mn and β-Mn were coprecipitated during aging before the α matrix fully transformed to β-Mn.

claim: At 550 °C, the α matrix had nearly fully transformed into β-Mn in the 17Al alloy after 6 h of aging, whereas the 19Al alloy required 10 h for the same transformation.
evidence_text: At $ { \bf 5 5 0 } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the α matrix had nearly fully transformed into β-Mn in the 17Al alloy after 6 h of aging. As for the 19Al alloy, it required 10 h.

claim: After 1 h of aging at 575 °C, a small amount of β-Mn precipitated along α grain boundaries and within α grains in the 17Al alloy, while in the 19Al alloy β-Mn was only observed along grain boundaries, and untransformed α remained even after 3 h.
evidence_text: After 1 h of aging at $ { 5 7 5 } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ a small amount of β-Mn precipitated along α grain boundaries and within α grains in the 17Al alloy, while β-Mn in the 19Al alloy could only be observed along α grain boundaries. Even extending the aging time to 3 h, the untransformed α matrix was still observed in the 19Al alloy.

claim: At 600 °C and above, the α had almost completely transformed into β-Mn in both alloys after just 1 h of aging, and numerous fine precipitates were distributed within the β-Mn after full transformation.
evidence_text: However, at temperatures of 600 °C and above, the α had almost completely transformed into β-Mn in both alloys after just 1 h of aging. Numerous fine precipitates distributed within the β-Mn were also observed in the 17Al and 19Al alloys after the α matrix had fully transformed into β-Mn.

claim: XRD patterns of the 15Al alloy aged at 550 °C for 1 h, the 17Al alloy aged at 575 °C for 1 h, and the 19Al alloy aged at 550 °C for 6 h showed both α-Mn and β-Mn diffraction peaks, indicating that α-Mn and β-Mn coprecipitated during aging before the α matrix fully transformed to β-Mn.
evidence_text: Based on the SE results, the 15Al alloy aged at $5 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 1 h, 17Al alloy aged at $5 7 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 1 h, and 19Al alloy aged at 550 °C for 6 h were selected for XRD tests, and the results are shown in Fig. 10. Both α-Mn and β-Mn diffraction peaks were found in these XRD patterns. This indicates that α-Mn and β-Mn were coprecipitated during aging before the α matrix fully transformed to β-Mn.

claim: After aging at 600 °C for 6 h, the α matrix completely disappeared and abundant near-equiaxed β-Mn formed; fine particles evenly distributed at β-Mn interfaces exhibited an FCC structure and were rich in Cu, depleted in Fe and Mn, and slightly poor in Al, identified as FCC Cu-rich phase, while β-Mn was poor in Cu.
evidence_text: the α matrix completely disappeared and abundant near equiaxed β-Mn formed in the 15Al alloy after aging at 600 °C for 6 h. Besides, some fine particles are evenly distributed at the interfaces between the β-Mn. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns shown in Fig. 11 (b) indicate that the fine particles exhibited an FCC crystalline structure, with FCC twins frequently observed within the particles. … Fig. 11 (c) shows that the FCC phase was rich in Cu, depleted in Fe and Mn, and slightly poor in Al.

claim: In the 17Al alloy aged at 575 °C for 6 h, TEM revealed a large amount of fine FCC Cu-rich particles distributed at β-Mn interfaces, with FCC twins within the Cu-rich particles, and SAED/HRTEM confirmed the β-Mn and FCC Cu-rich phase interface.
evidence_text: a large amount of fine particles were distributed at the interfaces of β-Mn ... EDS elemental mapping results indicated that these particles were the FCC Cu-rich phase ... FCC twin structure was also observed within the Cu-rich particles. Fig. 12 (c) provides the SAED pattern and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of β-Mn. Fig. 12 (d) shows the HRTEM image of the interface between the β-Mn and FCC Cu-rich phase.

claim: The fracture toughness (KIC) of the hardest samples decreased with increasing Al content: 8.6 MPa⋅m^1/2 for 15Al, 6.6 MPa⋅m^1/2 for 17Al, and 3.5 MPa⋅m^1/2 for 19Al, corresponding to average crack lengths of 18 μm, 22 μm, and 35 μm.
evidence_text: The average crack lengths resulting from the hardness measurements were 18 μm for 15Al alloy, 22 μm for 17Al alloy, and 35 μm for 19Al alloy. Therefore, the calculated K_IC values … were 8.6 MPa⋅m1/2, 6.6 MPa⋅m1/2, and 3.5 MPa⋅m1/2, respectively. This indicates that the K_IC of ultra-hard Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels decreased with increasing Al content.

claim: In the 15Al alloy (Fe-44.6Mn-35.2Al-15.0Cu-5.2 at.%), the α matrix had been almost completely transformed into β-Mn within 3 h of aging at 525 °C.
evidence_text: within 3 h of aging at 525 °C, the α matrix had been almost completely transformed into β-Mn (Fig. 7).

claim: For the 17Al and 19Al alloys, complete transformation from α to β-Mn was only completed after 6 h and 10 h when aging at 550 °C, respectively.
evidence_text: for the 17Al and 19Al alloys, this transformation was only completed after 6 h and 10 h when aging at 550 °C, respectively (Figs. 8 and 9).

claim: The decrease in precipitation kinetics of β-Mn with increasing Al content is attributed to the enrichment of Al atoms around β-Mn hindering its growth and the stabilizing effect of Al on the D03 phase, which must be depleted before β-Mn formation.
evidence_text: 1) The β-Mn is a Mn-rich phase, and its nucleation and growth must exclude large amounts of other atoms to the surroundings... the increase in Al content resulted in the enrichment of more Al atoms around β-Mn, which would hinder its further precipitation and growth. 2)... the precipitation of the D03 phase... the precipitation and growth of β-Mn were accompanied by the depletion of the D03 phase. Since the increase in Al content would lead to an increase in the amount and size of the D03 phase and also stabilize it, this might hinder the nucleation and growth of β-Mn

claim: The microstructure evolutions during aging treatments of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels followed this sequence: α → α + FCC Cu-rich phase + α-Mn + β-Mn + BCC phase → β-Mn + BCC phase + FCC Cu-rich phase.
evidence_text: The microstructure evolutions during aging treatments of the Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels followed this sequence: α → α + FCC Cu-rich phase + α-Mn + β-Mn + BCC phase→ β-Mn + BCC phase + FCC Cu-rich phase.

claim: In 15Al aged at 650 °C for 3 h the volume fraction of FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases was 3.9% with Vickers hardness 852.3 HV; in 17Al aged at 650 °C for 10 h the fraction was 4.7% with hardness 833.2 HV; in 19Al aged at 650 °C for 6 h the fraction was 6.5% with hardness 810.9 HV.
evidence_text: the volume fraction of FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases of the 15Al alloy after aging at 650 °C for 3 h was 3.9 %, and the Vickers hardness of this sample was 852.3 HV. The volume fraction of these precipitates of the 17Al alloy after aging at 650 °C for 10 h was 4.7 %, and the average Vickers hardness of the alloy was 833.2 HV. However, after the 19Al alloy aged at 650 °C for 6 h, the volume fraction of FCC Cu-rich and BCC precipitates reached 6.5 %, and its Vickers hardness was only 810.9 HV.

claim: The fracture toughness KIC of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels decreased with increasing Al content.
evidence_text: the KIC of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu lowdensity steels decreased with increasing Al content.

claim: The decrease in KIC with increasing Al content is due to the increase in intrinsic hardness of β-Mn and the reduction in volume fraction of the more ductile FCC Cu-rich phase.
evidence_text: Firstly, the intrinsic hardness of β-Mn gradually increased with increasing Al content, and the KIC should be deteriorating accordingly. Moreover, the FCC phase exhibited relatively low intrinsic hardness but is more ductile... Therefore, the decrease in ductile FCC Cu-rich phase due to increasing Al content led to the decrease in fracture toughness of ultra-hard alloys.

claim: Fig. 15 shows EBSD FSD images, IPF, phase maps, and EDS mapping for the 15Al (525 °C for 3 h), 17Al (525 °C for 10 h) and 19Al (525 °C for 10 h) alloys, revealing β-Mn, BCC, α-Mn, and FCC phases.
evidence_text: Fig. 15. EBSD Forescatter detector (FSD) images, inverse pole figures (IPF), phase maps, and EDS mapping for the 15Al (525 °C for 3 h), 17Al (525 °C for 10 h) and 19Al (525 °C for 10 h) alloys.

claim: As shown in Fig. 16, the Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels achieve a maximum specific hardness exceeding 140 HV·cm³/g, whereas existing steels and M/HEAs generally do not exceed 800 HV in hardness and exhibit specific hardness below 120 HV·cm³/g.
evidence_text: Fig. 16 compares the specific hardness (hardness/density, HV/ρ) of our Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with various low-density steels, other steels, pure iron, and medium/high entropy alloys. The hardness of existing steels and M/HEAs generally does not exceed 800 HV and coupled with the higher densities. This results in their specific hardness usually below $1 2 0 \\ { \\mathrm { H V } } { \\cdot } { \\mathrm { c m } } ^ { 3 } / { \\mathrm { g } } .$ In contrast, our Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels achieve a maximum specific hardness exceeding 140 $\\scriptstyle { \\mathrm { H V } } \\cdot { \\mathrm { c m } } ^ { 3 } / { \\mathrm { g } }$ while maintaining low density.

claim: The alloys dramatically increase hardness within short-time aging, saving fabrication time and costs, and their low density and flexible hardness enable 'one type of material, multiple uses' in lightweight structural materials.
evidence_text: the Fe-Mn-Al-Cu alloys are able to increase the hardness dramatically within short-time aging, saving fabrication time and costs. In addition, its low density and flexible hardness can realize "one type of material, multiple uses" under the condition of meeting the application premise of lightweight structural materials.
